{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",

runContext.design_guide:
# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — Current Codebase Version: 1.1 — Scope: branding, tokens, article content components, routing/import rules, accessibility, and safe one-shot code generation for the current React Router codebase. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form content that is visually consistent, inclusive, accessible, and compatible with this repository without guessing. Applies to: marketing pages, articles, programs/events pages, community resources, dashboards/apps, and any new long-form content. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette and code tokens MLAI’s current visual system combines modern tech teal tokens with bold editorial/brutalist article surfaces. The codebase uses Tailwind utilities, CSS variables, and article components that already encode most of the preferred styling. Use these tokens/classes where they already appear in the codebase: | Token / class | Role | |---|---| | `--brand` | Primary bright teal surface/accent. Used for bullets, accent borders, and focus-like emphasis. | | `--brand-ink` | Teal ink for links and hover states on light backgrounds. | | `--accent` | Charcoal/dark text on bright teal surfaces. | | `--soft` | Soft teal panel background. | | `#ff3d00` | Editorial orange surface used by article cards/callouts. | | `#4b1bd1` | Editorial purple surface used by article cards/callouts. | | `#ffe900` | Editorial yellow surface used by audience/event cards. | | `#f8f3e8` | Warm beige editorial panel, used by author bio surfaces. | | `bg-transparent` | Required neutral article wrapper/background class for new content wrappers. | #### Guardrails Normal text should remain WCAG-aware: - On light/transparent article backgrounds: - Use `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, or `text-black`. - Use `text-[--brand-ink]` for links and subtle brand emphasis. - Do not use low-contrast bright teal as body text. - On bright teal surfaces: - Use dark text such as `text-[--accent]` or `text-gray-900`. - Do not put body-sized white text on `--brand`. - On orange or purple editorial surfaces: - Use white text. - Prefer existing components such as `` and `` instead of recreating the styling. - On yellow editorial surfaces: - Use black text. - For wrappers: - Use `bg-transparent`. - Do **not** add `bg-white` to new article content wrappers. Links: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse articles ``` External links should use normal anchors with `target="_blank"` and `rel="noopener noreferrer"` when opening a new tab. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape Typography: - Article content is rendered inside the repository’s article/prose system. - Do not add a duplicate `

` inside article content files. - Start article body sections at `

` unless a component already renders the section heading. - Use semantic heading order. Spacing: - Use Tailwind spacing utilities. - Common article block spacing: - `my-8` - `my-12` - `mt-8 mb-8` - `mt-10` - `space-y-6` Shape: - Cards and panels commonly use: - `rounded-lg` - `rounded-2xl` - `rounded-3xl` - `rounded-[22px]` - `rounded-[28px]` - `rounded-[32px]` Borders: - Neutral article panels commonly use: - `border border-gray-300` - `border border-gray-400` - `border-l-4` --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus Focus: - Always keep visible focus styles. - Existing CTA/link patterns use Tailwind focus utilities such as: - `focus:outline-none` - `focus:ring-2` - `focus-visible:ring-2` - `focus-visible:ring-offset-2` Motion: - Keep transitions subtle: - `transition` - `transition-colors` - `transition-transform` - `duration-200` - `hover:-translate-y-1` Do not remove keyboard focus outlines from interactive elements. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity Applies globally: every screen and long-form page uses a clear, practical, encouraging voice. - Use AU English. - Define acronyms once. - No hype or guarantees. - Do not imply official endorsement by government, universities, vendors, or community partners unless explicitly true. - When discussing AI, be clear about limitations, risks, and the need to verify sources. - When discussing startups, avoid financial advice language; keep it educational and practical. - Do not add medical, legal, or broad disclaimers unless the specific article brief explicitly asks for them. --- ## 2) Framework, routing, imports & theming ### 2.1 Framework detection This repository is a React Router codebase. Authoritative stack: - React: `19.2.1` - Routing: React Router v7 - Links: import from `react-router` and use the `to` prop - Styling: Tailwind CSS - Language: TypeScript - Component files: `.tsx` - Build tool: Vite - Package manager: Bun Never use Next.js-only APIs: - No `next/link` - No `next/image` - No `next/dynamic` - No `'use client'` - No `'use server'` - No `getStaticProps` - No `getServerSideProps` - No `generateStaticParams` - No `export const metadata` - No `next/font` Correct link pattern: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles ``` --- ### 2.2 Import conventions Use the repository alias prefix `~/` for app imports. Preferred examples: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Default-export components must be imported as defaults: ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA' import ArticleResourceCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` Named-export components must be imported with braces: ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Both of these are valid for `QuoteBlock` because the file exports named and default: ```tsx import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` or ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Do not import missing components: - Do not use `ArticleResourceDownload` - Do not use `ArticleSummaryCard` --- ### 2.3 Tailwind setup The repository uses Tailwind with the typography plugin. Current config pattern: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography' import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss' export default { content: ['./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}'], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [ typography, ], } satisfies Config ``` Do not assume custom Tailwind theme aliases exist. Prefer literal utilities and existing CSS variables/classes used in the codebase: ```tsx
...
``` Use `bg-transparent`, not `bg-white`, for new wrapper surfaces. --- ## 3) Article architecture rules These are critical for one-shot code generation. ### 3.1 Article content files Article content files live under: ```txt app/articles/content//.tsx ``` Article content files should export a React component and return a fragment or transparent wrapper: ```tsx export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

...

...

) } ``` or: ```tsx export default function ArticleContent() { return (
...
) } ``` ### 3.2 Do not wrap content in `ArticleLayout` Article content files must **not** import or render `ArticleLayout`. The route/article rendering system already handles layout, header, metadata, breadcrumbs, author surfaces, related articles, and other global article chrome. Do not do this: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( ... ) } ``` Correct: ```tsx export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <> ... ) } ``` ### 3.3 Do not duplicate headers If the article route/layout already renders the title, breadcrumb, date, author, or hero header, do not add a second article header inside the body. Avoid: ```tsx

{{TOPIC}}

``` Use: ```tsx

Overview

``` ### 3.4 Path consistency When adding or editing article registry and SEO config entries: - The registry slug path must match the SEO config path. - The public URL should resolve as `/articles/`. - Keep internal links consistent with `resolveArticleRouteSlug` behaviour where applicable. --- ## 4) Global component rules ### Design system first Use existing article/design components instead of raw custom UI for reusable blocks. Required patterns: - Persona / “Who is this for?” cards: use ``. - Callouts / quotes / highlighted notes: use ``. - Images: use ``. - Step/checklist sequences: use ``. - FAQs: use ``. - References/resources: use `` or ``. - Table of contents placeholder: use ``. Do not create raw card grids with ad-hoc `div` borders/shadows when a matching component exists. ### Variants Only use variant values that exist in TypeScript types. Allowed `AudienceGrid` variants: ```txt orange | purple | yellow ``` Allowed `QuoteBlock` variants: ```txt purple | orange ``` Allowed `ArticleStepList` accents: ```txt teal | indigo ``` Do not use: ```tsx variant="blue" variant="brand" ``` ### Images Use `` for article body images. Do not use placeholder image services such as `placehold.co`. Use explicit real URLs or safe placeholders such as: ```tsx ``` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes ### 5.1 Article image Use `ArticleImageBlock` for article images. ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Props: ```ts type ArticleImageBlockProps = { src: string alt: string width?: number height?: number containerClassName?: string imageClassName?: string caption?: string } ``` Notes: - Do not use raw `` for article body imagery. - Do not use `placehold.co`. - Keep alt text descriptive and specific. --- ### 5.2 AudienceGrid Use for persona cards, “who this is for”, and audience segmentation blocks. ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Allowed variants: ```txt orange | purple | yellow ``` Do not use raw card grids for persona sections. --- ### 5.3 QuoteBlock Use for callouts, key takeaways, quotes, and highlighted guidance. ```tsx import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock'

Start with the smallest useful workflow, test it with real users, and document what changed before scaling the approach.

``` Orange variant: ```tsx

Keep the first version narrow enough that a team can review outputs and catch mistakes quickly.

``` Allowed variants: ```txt purple | orange ``` Do not use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"`. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList Use for numbered steps or action plans. ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Allowed accents: ```txt teal | indigo ``` Prefer `accent="teal"` for brand-aligned steps. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ Use for FAQ sections. ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` Props: ```ts type ArticleFAQItem = { q?: string question?: string a?: ReactNode answer?: ReactNode } ``` Notes: - `heading` defaults to `Frequently Asked Questions`. - `anchorId` defaults to `faq`. --- ### 5.6 ArticleTocPlaceholder Use where the article enhancer should inject a generated table of contents. ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` No-margin variant: ```tsx ``` Do not manually build a custom table of contents unless specifically required. --- ### 5.7 ArticleReferences Use for source lists, official resources, guides, and further reading. ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` Known category styles: ```txt government | guide | analysis | industry | watchlist ``` Unknown category strings fall back to neutral styling. --- ### 5.8 ArticleResourceList Use for lists of downloadable or linked resources. ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` Badge tones are constrained by the internal `Badge` variants: ```txt red | purple | black | yellow ``` Do not import `ArticleResourceDownload`; it is not present in the codebase. --- ### 5.9 ArticleResourceCTA Use for large editorial resource CTAs when a strong visual panel is needed. ```tsx import ArticleResourceCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' ``` Allowed accents: ```txt purple | cyan | orange ``` Keep CTA copy generic. Do not hard-code product-specific names unless the task explicitly asks for them. --- ### 5.10 ArticleCompanyCTA Use for a simple centred CTA. ```tsx import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Props: ```ts export interface ArticleCompanyCTAProps { title: string body: string buttonText: string buttonHref: string note?: string id?: string className?: string } ``` --- ### 5.11 ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA Use for image-led CTA panels. ```tsx import ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA' ', alt: 'People reviewing AI workflow ideas together', }} /> ``` Do not use `placehold.co` for the image. --- ### 5.12 ArticleCardBySlug Use for related article cards when the article exists in the registry. ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' ``` Allowed variants: ```txt default | compact ``` The component resolves article title, description, route slug, image, date, and author from the article registry. --- ### 5.13 ArticleFooterNav Use for simple footer navigation inside article content only when needed. ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` Defaults: ```txt backHref = /articles backLabel = ← Back to Articles topHref = # ``` --- ### 5.14 AuthorBio Most article layout/route surfaces already handle authors. Only add `AuthorBio` manually when the task explicitly requires an in-body author panel. ```tsx import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` Avoid duplicate author sections if the layout already renders author information. --- ### 5.15 HeroSection Use `HeroSection` for page-level non-article headers, not for article body content that is already wrapped by the article route. ```tsx import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Allowed variants: ```txt yellow | beige | white ``` For new article content, do not add a second hero/header. --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist Copy into every PR: - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus is visible on all interactive elements. - [ ] Links use `Link` from `react-router` with `to` for internal navigation. - [ ] External links use `` and include `rel="noopener noreferrer"` when opening a new tab. - [ ] Heading order is semantic. - [ ] Article body does not include a duplicate `

# Company Overview: MLAI

> This document provides context for AI-generated articles. It ensures content aligns with the company's brand, audience, and goals.

---

## 1. Company Identity

**What we are (plain-English):**  
MLAI Aus Inc is an Australian not-for-profit community for people building, learning and working with AI and machine learning. It runs events, meetups, hackathons, workshops, community programs, founder tools and related software/services to help Australia’s AI ecosystem grow through practical learning, real-world projects and meaningful professional connections.

**Industry/Sector:** Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Technology Community, Startup Ecosystem, Education/Events  
**Type:** Not-for-profit community organisation; event organiser; educational/community program operator; software/founder tools provider  
**Founded:** [To be filled]  
**Location/Market:** Australia, with events and programs hosted across Australia; broader AI community impact “in Australia and beyond” where relevant

---

## 2. Mission & North Star

**Mission Statement:**  
MLAI’s mission is to make AI accessible, fun and impactful while empowering the Australian AI community. The organisation exists to foster a vibrant community of AI builders, learners, founders, researchers, professionals and students through meetups, hackathons, educational programs and practical collaboration.

**Vision/North Star:**  
To help make Australia an amazing home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs by building a thriving AI ecosystem through community, events, hackathons, founder support and educational programs.

**Key Values:**
- **Accessibility:** Make AI approachable for learners, builders and professionals from diverse backgrounds.
- **Practical impact:** Focus on real-world applications, working prototypes and useful outcomes rather than abstract AI hype.
- **Community contribution:** Recognise meaningful contributions through community systems such as Roo Points.
- **Collaboration:** Bring together developers, researchers, founders, clinicians, students, mentors, sponsors and industry partners.
- **Learning by doing:** Use hackathons, workshops, lightning talks and events to help people build skills through hands-on participation.
- **Ecosystem building:** Support the growth of Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup communities.

---

## 3. Target Audience

### Primary Audience

Use this company as the “home base” in articles for readers such as:

- **AI builders and practitioners:** Developers, data scientists, AI engineers and technical operators who want to learn, build projects, attend events, meet collaborators and apply AI to real-world problems.
- **Students and early-career learners:** People new to AI or machine learning who want accessible events, workshops, community support and practical ways to get involved.
- **Startup founders and AI entrepreneurs:** Founders building AI-enabled companies who need community, visibility, investor-network building, founder updates, mentoring and practical support before fundraising.
- **Researchers and technical professionals:** People working in AI, machine learning, data science or adjacent technical fields who want to connect with peers and contribute expertise.
- **Domain experts and professionals:** Clinicians, healthcare professionals, energy experts, safety professionals and others who can help shape practical AI solutions in specific sectors.
- **Sponsors, partners and ecosystem leaders:** Organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs.

### Pain Points to Address

- AI can feel inaccessible, overhyped or difficult to apply in practical settings.
- Builders and learners often lack a supportive local community where they can meet collaborators and mentors.
- Founders may start raising money too late and need help building their investor network earlier.
- Professionals from non-technical domains may want to explore AI but need structured, collaborative environments.
- Hackathon participants need real problems, clear judging criteria, mentors and opportunities to prototype useful solutions.
- Volunteers and community contributors need transparent recognition for meaningful work.
- Sponsors and partners need credible ways to engage with Australia’s AI talent and startup ecosystem.

### Goals They’re Trying to Achieve

- Learn AI and machine learning in a practical, community-supported environment.
- Meet collaborators, mentors, founders, researchers, developers and industry partners.
- Build prototypes and real-world AI/software projects through hackathons.
- Participate in events, meetups, workshops and lightning talks.
- Grow a startup network and prepare for future fundraising.
- Contribute to the community and earn recognition through Roo Points.
- Support or sponsor Australia’s AI ecosystem in a credible, useful way.

---

## 4. Product/Service Offerings

Think of the company’s offering as:

### Core Offerings

1. **AI and machine learning events:**  
   MLAI hosts events, meetups and workshops across Australia, including AI Week activities, startup-focused sessions and practical community gatherings. The events calendar is available at `/events`.

2. **Hackathons and challenge programs:**  
   MLAI runs or promotes AI/software hackathons focused on real-world problems. Examples from the website include:
   - **Watt The Hack:** Energy and AI hackathon for a cleaner, smarter energy future.
   - **MedHack: Frontiers:** Healthcare AI hackathon focused on medical innovation and real healthcare bottlenecks.
   - **eSafety Hackathon:** Online safety-focused innovation challenge.
   Hackathon information appears at `/hackathons` and specific pages such as `/medhack`.

3. **Community programs and Slack participation:**  
   MLAI operates community spaces including Slack/Discord-style collaboration channels. Members can participate, introduce themselves, support founders, share updates and volunteer.

4. **Roo and Roo Points:**  
   Roo is the MLAI Slack helper for getting involved, checking points, booking coworking, viewing rewards and requesting community perks. Roo Points are MLAI’s internal thank-you system for meaningful contributions. They can be used for perks such as coworking, event tickets, office hours, merch and other Roo-managed rewards.

5. **Founder Tools / founder support:**  
   MLAI offers founder-oriented support that helps founders create updates, sharpen their signal and start building an investor network before raising. The website specifically says: “Most founders start raising when it’s too late.”

6. **Educational programs, workshops and resources:**  
   MLAI runs educational programs, workshops, talks, templates, guides and community learning materials for general educational and informational purposes.

7. **Software and project services:**  
   MLAI operates websites, founder tools, software, project services and connected-account products. Privacy documentation mentions products such as **Vibe Raising** for founder/investor updates and **Vibe Marketing** for AI-assisted marketing strategy and content workflows. Public-facing positioning for these products should be kept conservative unless a dedicated product page is provided.

8. **Sponsorship and partnerships:**  
   MLAI offers sponsorship opportunities for organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs. The sponsorship page is `/sponsors`.

### Key Differentiators

- **Community-first AI ecosystem:** MLAI is positioned as a not-for-profit community, not just an event company or software vendor.
- **Australia-specific focus:** The organisation is explicitly focused on making Australia a strong home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs.
- **Practical, hands-on format:** Hackathons, workshops and events are framed around building real solutions to real problems.
- **Cross-disciplinary participation:** MLAI brings together technical builders, founders, researchers, clinicians, students, mentors, sponsors and industry professionals.
- **Contribution recognition system:** Roo Points provide an internal mechanism for recognising meaningful community contributions and converting them into practical perks.
- **Founder support embedded in community:** Founder Tools help startups create updates and build investor networks earlier, within a broader AI community context.

---

## 5. Proof Points & Credibility

**Trust Signals (pick 1-2 per article, max):**
- MLAI is a not-for-profit organisation based in Australia.
- MLAI has “over 10,000 members from diverse backgrounds,” including data scientists, AI developers, healthcare professionals and students.
- MLAI runs monthly lightning talks and large-scale hackathons.
- MLAI’s events are described as “always oversubscribed,” reflecting strong demand for practical AI applications and professional connections.
- MLAI partners with organisations that share its vision for Australia’s AI future.
- MedHack: Frontiers is run with MYMI, a student-led organisation affiliated with Monash University.
- MedHack: Frontiers is hosted in connection with Monash University Clayton Campus.
- MLAI provides community spaces, events, workshops, hackathons, founder tools, software, project services and educational programs.
- Roo lives inside the MLAI Slack workspace and helps manage community participation, rewards and requests.

**What NOT to claim:**
- Do **not** claim MLAI guarantees jobs, funding, investment, introductions, prizes, rankings, commercial outcomes, technical outcomes, educational outcomes or business success.
- Do **not** present Roo Points as money, wages, a cash-value system, an exchange rate or employment compensation.
- Do **not** imply that earning Roo Points automatically guarantees an MLAI role, event ownership or paid work.
- Do **not** imply MLAI endorses every sponsor, partner, job listing, investment opportunity, community post or third-party offer unless explicitly stated.
- Do **not** present MLAI articles, events or hackathon content as legal, financial, tax, investment, medical, engineering, energy-market, environmental, policy, cybersecurity, accounting or other professional advice.
- Do **not** overclaim specific sponsor names, awards, certifications, funding raised, participant numbers for specific events or commercial results unless provided.
- Do **not** describe MLAI as a university, venture fund, accelerator or government body unless specifically supported by source content.
- Do **not** claim specific product capabilities for Vibe Raising or Vibe Marketing beyond what is described in the privacy policy unless a dedicated product page confirms them.

---

## 6. CTA Guidance

### How to Describe the Company in One Line (choose one per article)

- “MLAI is an Australian not-for-profit community helping builders, founders and learners apply AI through events, hackathons and practical programs.”
- “MLAI brings together Australia’s AI builders, researchers, students and entrepreneurs through hands-on events, hackathons and community support.”
- “MLAI is a community for people building and learning with AI in Australia.”
- “MLAI supports Australia’s AI ecosystem with meetups, hackathons, founder tools and community programs.”

### Primary CTAs (Use ONE per article, aligned to topic)

**For beginners/learners:**
> “New to AI or looking for a practical way in? Browse upcoming MLAI events and join a community of builders and learners.”  
> Target: `/events`

**For builders/practitioners:**
> “Want to build with other AI practitioners? Explore upcoming MLAI hackathons and community events.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` or `/events`

**For hackathon readers:**
> “Interested in solving real-world challenges with AI? Check the latest MLAI hackathon opportunities.”  
> Target: `/hackathons`

**For healthcare/medtech readers:**
> “If you’re interested in healthcare AI, explore MedHack: Frontiers and see how clinicians, builders and mentors work on real healthcare bottlenecks.”  
> Target: `/medhack`

**For startup founders:**
> “If you’re a founder, start building your investor network early with MLAI’s founder tools and community support.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` section mentioning Founder Tools, or [To be filled] if a dedicated Founder Tools URL exists

**For community contributors:**
> “Want to get more involved in the MLAI community? Meet Roo and learn how Roo Points recognise useful contributions.”  
> Target: `/roo`

**For decision-makers/leaders:**
> “Looking to connect with Australia’s AI builders, researchers and entrepreneurs? Explore MLAI’s events and partnership opportunities.”  
> Target: `/events` or `/sponsors`

**For partners/sponsors:**
> “Partner with MLAI to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs building the future of AI.”  
> Target: `/sponsors`

### CTA Rules

- Use **ONE CTA per article**, ideally as a natural final line or short aside.
- Match the CTA to the article topic:
  - Learning/general AI → `/events`
  - Building/prototyping → `/hackathons`
  - Healthcare AI → `/medhack`
  - Community participation/rewards → `/roo`
  - Sponsorship/partnership → `/sponsors`
- Keep CTAs helpful and community-oriented, not sales-heavy.
- Avoid implying a guaranteed outcome from attending, joining, sponsoring or participating.
- Use MLAI’s own terminology: “events,” “hackathons,” “community,” “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “Roo Points,” “Slack,” “perks,” “practical AI applications.”
- When referencing Founder Tools, avoid inventing a URL unless confirmed. Use a generic CTA or mark the URL as [To be filled].

### What NOT to do with CTAs

- Do not use aggressive sales language such as “Buy now,” “Dominate AI,” “Get guaranteed investors,” or “Secure funding today.”
- Do not push Roo Points as a paid currency, job pathway or income opportunity.
- Do not promise mentorship, paid work, sponsorship, investor introductions or prizes unless specifically tied to an event page.
- Do not direct every reader to sponsor pages; sponsorship CTAs are only for partner/business-leader content.
- Do not position MLAI as providing professional advice, medical advice, fundraising advice or investment advice.

---

## 7. Tone & Voice Guidelines

**Brand Voice:** Practical, community-first, builder-friendly, optimistic and accessible. MLAI’s tone is energetic and inviting, but should stay grounded in real-world outcomes rather than hype.

### Tone Attributes

- **Practical over hype:** Focus on building, learning, prototyping and solving real problems.
- **Community-first:** Emphasise collaboration, contribution, participation and shared progress.
- **Accessible but credible:** Make AI feel approachable without oversimplifying technical or sector-specific issues.
- **Optimistic and action-oriented:** Encourage readers to get involved, meet people, build something and contribute.
- **Cross-disciplinary:** Welcome non-technical experts as well as technical builders.
- **Australian ecosystem-focused:** Frame MLAI as supporting Australia’s AI and machine learning community.

### Do’s

- Use inclusive language such as “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “students,” “clinicians,” “researchers,” “mentors” and “community.”
- Use practical phrases from the site, such as:
  - “real-world challenges”
  - “practical AI applications”
  - “meaningful professional connections”
  - “help the community”
  - “get involved”
  - “build practical AI and software projects”
  - “real solutions to real bottlenecks”
- Use Australian English spelling where applicable: “organisation,” “recognise,” “programs” is acceptable because the site uses “programs,” “optimise” where relevant.
- Explain AI concepts in plain English before going deeper.
- Keep article advice educational and informational unless MLAI has explicitly agreed to provide professional services in that context.
- When writing about hackathons, emphasise teamwork, mentors, prototypes, judging criteria and real-world problem solving.
- When writing about Roo Points, include the internal-use caveat: they are recognition points, not money or wages.

### Don’ts

- Don’t use inflated AI hype such as “revolutionary,” “world-changing,” “unprecedented,” or “disrupt everything” unless quoting event copy directly.
- Don’t imply that AI alone solves complex healthcare, energy, safety or policy problems.
- Don’t make unsupported claims about event attendance, sponsor names, awards, university affiliations or participant outcomes.
- Don’t treat MLAI like a commercial SaaS company first; its primary identity is community/not-for-profit.
- Don’t overuse corporate jargon such as “synergise,” “unlock exponential value,” or “leverage AI transformation” without concrete meaning.
- Don’t write as if MLAI provides legal, financial, investment, medical or fundraising advice.
- Don’t frame community participation as transactional labour or employment.
- Don’t refer to Roo Points as cryptocurrency, tokens, cash, salary, wages or a marketplace currency.

---

## 8. Writing Guidance Summary

### Language Conventions

- **Locale:** Australian English preferred.
- **Preferred terms:** MLAI, MLAI Aus, Australian AI community, AI and machine learning, builders, learners, founders, entrepreneurs, researchers, students, clinicians, practitioners, community, events, meetups, workshops, hackathons, Roo, Roo Points, Slack, perks, founder updates, investor network.
- **Avoided terms:** Guaranteed funding, guaranteed jobs, guaranteed investment, cash value, wage, salary, cryptocurrency, official endorsement, professional advice, medical advice, legal advice, investment advice, “AI will replace,” “AI solves everything.”

### Content Rules

- Present MLAI as an Australian not-for-profit AI and machine learning community, not primarily as a SaaS vendor, agency, accelerator or investment platform.
- Ground articles in practical value: what readers can learn, build, contribute or connect around.
- Use proof points sparingly. The strongest available statistic is “over 10,000 members,” but avoid repeating it in every article.
- If discussing MedHack, describe it as a healthcare AI hackathon bringing clinicians, builders and optimists together to prototype solutions to healthcare bottlenecks.
- If discussing Roo, always clarify that Roo Points are an internal thank-you/contribution recognition system and have no cash value.
- If discussing sponsors, position sponsorship as supporting Australia’s AI community and connecting with talented developers, researchers and entrepreneurs.
- If discussing founder support, focus on early investor-network building, founder updates and sharpening signal. Do not promise fundraising success.
- Do not imply MLAI guarantees outcomes from events, programs, hackathons, sponsorships or community participation.
- Avoid inventing program details, dates, prizes, sponsor names, mentor names or URLs not present in source content.
- Keep CTAs soft, relevant and community-aligned: invite readers to explore events, hackathons, Roo or sponsorship pages depending on the article topic.

org_config_snapshot.design_guide:
# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — Current Codebase Version: 1.1 — Scope: branding, tokens, article content components, routing/import rules, accessibility, and safe one-shot code generation for the current React Router codebase. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form content that is visually consistent, inclusive, accessible, and compatible with this repository without guessing. Applies to: marketing pages, articles, programs/events pages, community resources, dashboards/apps, and any new long-form content. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette and code tokens MLAI’s current visual system combines modern tech teal tokens with bold editorial/brutalist article surfaces. The codebase uses Tailwind utilities, CSS variables, and article components that already encode most of the preferred styling. Use these tokens/classes where they already appear in the codebase: | Token / class | Role | |---|---| | `--brand` | Primary bright teal surface/accent. Used for bullets, accent borders, and focus-like emphasis. | | `--brand-ink` | Teal ink for links and hover states on light backgrounds. | | `--accent` | Charcoal/dark text on bright teal surfaces. | | `--soft` | Soft teal panel background. | | `#ff3d00` | Editorial orange surface used by article cards/callouts. | | `#4b1bd1` | Editorial purple surface used by article cards/callouts. | | `#ffe900` | Editorial yellow surface used by audience/event cards. | | `#f8f3e8` | Warm beige editorial panel, used by author bio surfaces. | | `bg-transparent` | Required neutral article wrapper/background class for new content wrappers. | #### Guardrails Normal text should remain WCAG-aware: - On light/transparent article backgrounds: - Use `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, or `text-black`. - Use `text-[--brand-ink]` for links and subtle brand emphasis. - Do not use low-contrast bright teal as body text. - On bright teal surfaces: - Use dark text such as `text-[--accent]` or `text-gray-900`. - Do not put body-sized white text on `--brand`. - On orange or purple editorial surfaces: - Use white text. - Prefer existing components such as `` and `` instead of recreating the styling. - On yellow editorial surfaces: - Use black text. - For wrappers: - Use `bg-transparent`. - Do **not** add `bg-white` to new article content wrappers. Links: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse articles ``` External links should use normal anchors with `target="_blank"` and `rel="noopener noreferrer"` when opening a new tab. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape Typography: - Article content is rendered inside the repository’s article/prose system. - Do not add a duplicate `

` inside article content files. - Start article body sections at `

` unless a component already renders the section heading. - Use semantic heading order. Spacing: - Use Tailwind spacing utilities. - Common article block spacing: - `my-8` - `my-12` - `mt-8 mb-8` - `mt-10` - `space-y-6` Shape: - Cards and panels commonly use: - `rounded-lg` - `rounded-2xl` - `rounded-3xl` - `rounded-[22px]` - `rounded-[28px]` - `rounded-[32px]` Borders: - Neutral article panels commonly use: - `border border-gray-300` - `border border-gray-400` - `border-l-4` --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus Focus: - Always keep visible focus styles. - Existing CTA/link patterns use Tailwind focus utilities such as: - `focus:outline-none` - `focus:ring-2` - `focus-visible:ring-2` - `focus-visible:ring-offset-2` Motion: - Keep transitions subtle: - `transition` - `transition-colors` - `transition-transform` - `duration-200` - `hover:-translate-y-1` Do not remove keyboard focus outlines from interactive elements. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity Applies globally: every screen and long-form page uses a clear, practical, encouraging voice. - Use AU English. - Define acronyms once. - No hype or guarantees. - Do not imply official endorsement by government, universities, vendors, or community partners unless explicitly true. - When discussing AI, be clear about limitations, risks, and the need to verify sources. - When discussing startups, avoid financial advice language; keep it educational and practical. - Do not add medical, legal, or broad disclaimers unless the specific article brief explicitly asks for them. --- ## 2) Framework, routing, imports & theming ### 2.1 Framework detection This repository is a React Router codebase. Authoritative stack: - React: `19.2.1` - Routing: React Router v7 - Links: import from `react-router` and use the `to` prop - Styling: Tailwind CSS - Language: TypeScript - Component files: `.tsx` - Build tool: Vite - Package manager: Bun Never use Next.js-only APIs: - No `next/link` - No `next/image` - No `next/dynamic` - No `'use client'` - No `'use server'` - No `getStaticProps` - No `getServerSideProps` - No `generateStaticParams` - No `export const metadata` - No `next/font` Correct link pattern: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles ``` --- ### 2.2 Import conventions Use the repository alias prefix `~/` for app imports. Preferred examples: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Default-export components must be imported as defaults: ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA' import ArticleResourceCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` Named-export components must be imported with braces: ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Both of these are valid for `QuoteBlock` because the file exports named and default: ```tsx import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` or ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Do not import missing components: - Do not use `ArticleResourceDownload` - Do not use `ArticleSummaryCard` --- ### 2.3 Tailwind setup The repository uses Tailwind with the typography plugin. Current config pattern: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography' import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss' export default { content: ['./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}'], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [ typography, ], } satisfies Config ``` Do not assume custom Tailwind theme aliases exist. Prefer literal utilities and existing CSS variables/classes used in the codebase: ```tsx
...

The route/article rendering system already handles layout, header, metadata, breadcrumbs, author surfaces, related articles, and other global article chrome. Do not do this: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( ... ) } ``` Correct: ```tsx export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <> ... ) } ``` ### 3.3 Do not duplicate headers If the article route/layout already renders the title, breadcrumb, date, author, or hero header, do not add a second article header inside the body. Do not use placeholder image services such as `placehold.co`. Do not hard-code product-specific names unless the task explicitly asks for them. --- ### 5.10 ArticleCompanyCTA Use for a simple centred CTA. ```tsx import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Props: ```ts export interface ArticleCompanyCTAProps { title: string body: string buttonText: string buttonHref: string note?: string id?: string className?: string } ``` --- ### 5.11 ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA Use for image-led CTA panels. ```tsx import ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyHighlightCTA' ', alt: 'People reviewing AI workflow ideas together', }} /> ``` Do not use `placehold.co` for the image. --- ### 5.12 ArticleCardBySlug Use for related article cards when the article exists in the registry. ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' ``` Allowed variants: ```txt default | compact ``` The component resolves article title, description, route slug, image, date, and author from the article registry. --- ### 5.13 ArticleFooterNav Use for simple footer navigation inside article content only when needed. ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` Defaults: ```txt backHref = /articles backLabel = ← Back to Articles topHref = # ``` --- ### 5.14 AuthorBio Most article layout/route surfaces already handle authors. Only add `AuthorBio` manually when the task explicitly requires an in-body author panel. ```tsx import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` Avoid duplicate author sections if the layout already renders author information. --- ### 5.15 HeroSection Use `HeroSection` for page-level non-article headers, not for article body content that is already wrapped by the article route. ```tsx import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Allowed variants: ```txt yellow | beige | white ``` For new article content, do not add a second hero/header. --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist Copy into every PR: - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus is visible on all interactive elements. - [ ] Links use `Link` from `react-router` with `to` for internal navigation. - [ ] External links use `` and include `rel="noopener noreferrer"` when opening a new tab. - [ ] Heading order is semantic. - [ ] Article body does not include a duplicate `

`. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Images have useful `alt` text, or are intentionally decorative. - [ ] Article body images use ``. - [ ] Persona cards use ``. - [ ] Callouts use ``. - [ ] No `placehold.co` URLs are used. - [ ] No Next.js-only APIs are used. --- ## 7) Copy & content rules Applies everywhere: marketing pages, events, dashboards, articles, and learning resources. - No testimonials, ratings, guarantees, or exaggerated claims unless specifically verified and approved. - Avoid “this will get you funded / hired / rich” claims. - Write concise summaries covering who, what, where, when, why, and how. - When referencing AI tools/models, be precise and state limits. - Where content may become outdated, include dates such as “as at ” when useful. - Do not add broad medical/legal disclaimers unless explicitly required by the task. - Keep CTA text generic, for example: - “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}” - “Explore next steps” - “Browse related guides” - “Open resource” --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes ### 8.1 Standard article body skeleton ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI -- One-Shot Article Generation Spec (React Router TSX) -- v2025-12-15 Purpose. Hand this document directly to an AI agent as a "system" / "developer" prompt so the agent can generate brand-consistent MLAI article content for this codebase in one shot. MLAI context. MLAI is a not-for-profit community organisation helping Australians learn about startups and artificial intelligence. Resources should be practical, inclusive, factual, and community-oriented. Avoid hype, guarantees, and unsupported claims. Output format: TypeScript React article content (`.tsx`) plus any required registry / SEO updates using the existing codebase structures. Engine: React 19 + React Router 7 + Vite + Tailwind CSS. Framework is authoritative: React Router, not Next.js. 0) Quick-start contract for the agent Task: Given a Topic, Title, Slug, Category Path, Description, Sections, References, optional FAQ items, optional images, and optional CTA, build a React Router article content module that adheres 100% to the rules below. Primary content file path: `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY_SLUG}}/{{ARTICLE_SLUG}}.tsx` If the repo already contains the target category folder, use it. If not, create it. Route path consistency: The article route path, registry slug, and SEO config path must all resolve to the same public article URL: `/articles/{{ARTICLE_SLUG}}` Do not create mismatched values such as one slug in the registry and a different slug in SEO config. Deliverables - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY_SLUG}}/{{ARTICLE_SLUG}}.tsx` - Registry update in the existing article registry file if required by the current codebase. - SEO config update in the existing SEO config file if required by the current codebase. - No PDF output. - No Next.js files or APIs. 1) Inputs you will receive Supply at minimum: - `topic`: `{{TOPIC}}` - `title`: article title - `articleSlug`: `{{ARTICLE_SLUG}}` - `categorySlug`: `{{CATEGORY_SLUG}}` - `description`: short SEO / card description - `date`: ISO date string, e.g. `2026-01-15` - `sections`: ordered article sections - `references`: authoritative references - optional `faqItems` - optional `heroImageUrl` - optional `imageBlocks` - optional `cta` Example input schema for your planner: ```ts { topic: "{{TOPIC}}", title: "{{TITLE}}", articleSlug: "{{ARTICLE_SLUG}}", categorySlug: "{{CATEGORY_SLUG}}", description: "{{DESCRIPTION}}", date: "{{DATE}}", sections: [ { heading: "Why {{TOPIC}} matters", body: ["Paragraph one.", "Paragraph two."] }, { heading: "A practical way to start", steps: [ "Clarify the goal.", "Choose one small experiment.", "Review what changed." ] } ], references: [ { title: "Australian Government - AI guidance", href: "https://www.industry.gov.au/", publisher: "Australian Government", description: "Official Australian Government information relevant to AI adoption.", category: "government" } ], faqItems: [ { question: "How should beginners approach {{TOPIC}}?", answer: "Start with one practical use case, learn the basics, and build confidence through small projects." } ], cta: { title: "Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}", body: "Explore related MLAI articles, templates, and community resources.", buttonText: "Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}", buttonHref: "/articles" } } ``` 2) Framework, routing, and file configuration Framework: - React 19.2.1 - React Router 7.10.0 - Vite - TypeScript - Tailwind CSS - Bun package manager Use these imports and patterns: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` Links must use `to`, not `href`: ```tsx Browse articles ``` Never use: - `next/link` - `next/image` - `next/dynamic` - `'use client'` - `'use server'` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - `generateStaticParams` - `export const metadata` - Next.js App Router patterns - ` in the article content. - Do not duplicate the page title. - Do not render breadcrumbs in the content. - Do not import `ArticleHeroHeader` in article content. - Do not import `HeroSection` in article content. - Do not import `AuthorBio` in article content. - Do not import `ArticleEnhancer` in article content. - Do not import `ArticleDisclaimer` unless explicitly requested by the task. 4) Import paths and component usage Use the alias import style used by the repo: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Use only components that exist in the codebase. org_config_snapshot.company_context:
# Company Overview: MLAI

> This document provides context for AI-generated articles. It ensures content aligns with the company's brand, audience, and goals.

---

## 1. Company Identity

**What we are (plain-English):**  
MLAI Aus Inc is an Australian not-for-profit community for people building, learning and working with AI and machine learning. It runs events, meetups, hackathons, workshops, community programs, founder tools and related software/services to help Australia’s AI ecosystem grow through practical learning, real-world projects and meaningful professional connections.

**Industry/Sector:** Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Technology Community, Startup Ecosystem, Education/Events  
**Type:** Not-for-profit community organisation; event organiser; educational/community program operator; software/founder tools provider  
**Founded:** [To be filled]  
**Location/Market:** Australia, with events and programs hosted across Australia; broader AI community impact “in Australia and beyond” where relevant

---

## 2. Mission & North Star

**Mission Statement:**  
MLAI’s mission is to make AI accessible, fun and impactful while empowering the Australian AI community. The organisation exists to foster a vibrant community of AI builders, learners, founders, researchers, professionals and students through meetups, hackathons, educational programs and practical collaboration.

**Vision/North Star:**  
To help make Australia an amazing home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs by building a thriving AI ecosystem through community, events, hackathons, founder support and educational programs.

**Key Values:**
- **Accessibility:** Make AI approachable for learners, builders and professionals from diverse backgrounds.
- **Practical impact:** Focus on real-world applications, working prototypes and useful outcomes rather than abstract AI hype.
- **Community contribution:** Recognise meaningful contributions through community systems such as Roo Points.
- **Collaboration:** Bring together developers, researchers, founders, clinicians, students, mentors, sponsors and industry partners.
- **Learning by doing:** Use hackathons, workshops, lightning talks and events to help people build skills through hands-on participation.
- **Ecosystem building:** Support the growth of Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup communities.

---

## 3. Target Audience

### Primary Audience

Use this company as the “home base” in articles for readers such as:

- **AI builders and practitioners:** Developers, data scientists, AI engineers and technical operators who want to learn, build projects, attend events, meet collaborators and apply AI to real-world problems.
- **Students and early-career learners:** People new to AI or machine learning who want accessible events, workshops, community support and practical ways to get involved.
- **Startup founders and AI entrepreneurs:** Founders building AI-enabled companies who need community, visibility, investor-network building, founder updates, mentoring and practical support before fundraising.
- **Researchers and technical professionals:** People working in AI, machine learning, data science or adjacent technical fields who want to connect with peers and contribute expertise.
- **Domain experts and professionals:** Clinicians, healthcare professionals, energy experts, safety professionals and others who can help shape practical AI solutions in specific sectors.
- **Sponsors, partners and ecosystem leaders:** Organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs.

### Pain Points to Address

- AI can feel inaccessible, overhyped or difficult to apply in practical settings.
- Builders and learners often lack a supportive local community where they can meet collaborators and mentors.
- Founders may start raising money too late and need help building their investor network earlier.
- Professionals from non-technical domains may want to explore AI but need structured, collaborative environments.
- Hackathon participants need real problems, clear judging criteria, mentors and opportunities to prototype useful solutions.
- Volunteers and community contributors need transparent recognition for meaningful work.
- Sponsors and partners need credible ways to engage with Australia’s AI talent and startup ecosystem.

### Goals They’re Trying to Achieve

- Learn AI and machine learning in a practical, community-supported environment.
- Meet collaborators, mentors, founders, researchers, developers and industry partners.
- Build prototypes and real-world AI/software projects through hackathons.
- Participate in events, meetups, workshops and lightning talks.
- Grow a startup network and prepare for future fundraising.
- Contribute to the community and earn recognition through Roo Points.
- Support or sponsor Australia’s AI ecosystem in a credible, useful way.

---

## 4. Product/Service Offerings

Think of the company’s offering as:

### Core Offerings

1. **AI and machine learning events:**  
   MLAI hosts events, meetups and workshops across Australia, including AI Week activities, startup-focused sessions and practical community gatherings. The events calendar is available at `/events`.

2. **Hackathons and challenge programs:**  
   MLAI runs or promotes AI/software hackathons focused on real-world problems. Examples from the website include:
   - **Watt The Hack:** Energy and AI hackathon for a cleaner, smarter energy future.
   - **MedHack: Frontiers:** Healthcare AI hackathon focused on medical innovation and real healthcare bottlenecks.
   - **eSafety Hackathon:** Online safety-focused innovation challenge.
   Hackathon information appears at `/hackathons` and specific pages such as `/medhack`.

3. **Community programs and Slack participation:**  
   MLAI operates community spaces including Slack/Discord-style collaboration channels. Members can participate, introduce themselves, support founders, share updates and volunteer.

4. **Roo and Roo Points:**  
   Roo is the MLAI Slack helper for getting involved, checking points, booking coworking, viewing rewards and requesting community perks. Roo Points are MLAI’s internal thank-you system for meaningful contributions. They can be used for perks such as coworking, event tickets, office hours, merch and other Roo-managed rewards.

5. **Founder Tools / founder support:**  
   MLAI offers founder-oriented support that helps founders create updates, sharpen their signal and start building an investor network before raising. The website specifically says: “Most founders start raising when it’s too late.”

6. **Educational programs, workshops and resources:**  
   MLAI runs educational programs, workshops, talks, templates, guides and community learning materials for general educational and informational purposes.

7. **Software and project services:**  
   MLAI operates websites, founder tools, software, project services and connected-account products. Privacy documentation mentions products such as **Vibe Raising** for founder/investor updates and **Vibe Marketing** for AI-assisted marketing strategy and content workflows. Public-facing positioning for these products should be kept conservative unless a dedicated product page is provided.

8. **Sponsorship and partnerships:**  
   MLAI offers sponsorship opportunities for organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs. The sponsorship page is `/sponsors`.

### Key Differentiators

- **Community-first AI ecosystem:** MLAI is positioned as a not-for-profit community, not just an event company or software vendor.
- **Australia-specific focus:** The organisation is explicitly focused on making Australia a strong home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs.
- **Practical, hands-on format:** Hackathons, workshops and events are framed around building real solutions to real problems.
- **Cross-disciplinary participation:** MLAI brings together technical builders, founders, researchers, clinicians, students, mentors, sponsors and industry professionals.
- **Contribution recognition system:** Roo Points provide an internal mechanism for recognising meaningful community contributions and converting them into practical perks.
- **Founder support embedded in community:** Founder Tools help startups create updates and build investor networks earlier, within a broader AI community context.

---

## 5. Proof Points & Credibility

**Trust Signals (pick 1-2 per article, max):**
- MLAI is a not-for-profit organisation based in Australia.
- MLAI has “over 10,000 members from diverse backgrounds,” including data scientists, AI developers, healthcare professionals and students.
- MLAI runs monthly lightning talks and large-scale hackathons.
- MLAI’s events are described as “always oversubscribed,” reflecting strong demand for practical AI applications and professional connections.
- MLAI partners with organisations that share its vision for Australia’s AI future.
- MedHack: Frontiers is run with MYMI, a student-led organisation affiliated with Monash University.
- MedHack: Frontiers is hosted in connection with Monash University Clayton Campus.
- MLAI provides community spaces, events, workshops, hackathons, founder tools, software, project services and educational programs.
- Roo lives inside the MLAI Slack workspace and helps manage community participation, rewards and requests.

**What NOT to claim:**
- Do **not** claim MLAI guarantees jobs, funding, investment, introductions, prizes, rankings, commercial outcomes, technical outcomes, educational outcomes or business success.
- Do **not** present Roo Points as money, wages, a cash-value system, an exchange rate or employment compensation.
- Do **not** imply that earning Roo Points automatically guarantees an MLAI role, event ownership or paid work.
- Do **not** imply MLAI endorses every sponsor, partner, job listing, investment opportunity, community post or third-party offer unless explicitly stated.
- Do **not** present MLAI articles, events or hackathon content as legal, financial, tax, investment, medical, engineering, energy-market, environmental, policy, cybersecurity, accounting or other professional advice.
- Do **not** overclaim specific sponsor names, awards, certifications, funding raised, participant numbers for specific events or commercial results unless provided.
- Do **not** describe MLAI as a university, venture fund, accelerator or government body unless specifically supported by source content.
- Do **not** claim specific product capabilities for Vibe Raising or Vibe Marketing beyond what is described in the privacy policy unless a dedicated product page confirms them.

---

## 6. CTA Guidance

### How to Describe the Company in One Line (choose one per article)

- “MLAI is an Australian not-for-profit community helping builders, founders and learners apply AI through events, hackathons and practical programs.”
- “MLAI brings together Australia’s AI builders, researchers, students and entrepreneurs through hands-on events, hackathons and community support.”
- “MLAI is a community for people building and learning with AI in Australia.”
- “MLAI supports Australia’s AI ecosystem with meetups, hackathons, founder tools and community programs.”

### Primary CTAs (Use ONE per article, aligned to topic)

**For beginners/learners:**
> “New to AI or looking for a practical way in? Browse upcoming MLAI events and join a community of builders and learners.”  
> Target: `/events`

**For builders/practitioners:**
> “Want to build with other AI practitioners? Explore upcoming MLAI hackathons and community events.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` or `/events`

**For hackathon readers:**
> “Interested in solving real-world challenges with AI? Check the latest MLAI hackathon opportunities.”  
> Target: `/hackathons`

**For healthcare/medtech readers:**
> “If you’re interested in healthcare AI, explore MedHack: Frontiers and see how clinicians, builders and mentors work on real healthcare bottlenecks.”  
> Target: `/medhack`

**For startup founders:**
> “If you’re a founder, start building your investor network early with MLAI’s founder tools and community support.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` section mentioning Founder Tools, or [To be filled] if a dedicated Founder Tools URL exists

**For community contributors:**
> “Want to get more involved in the MLAI community? Meet Roo and learn how Roo Points recognise useful contributions.”  
> Target: `/roo`

**For decision-makers/leaders:**
> “Looking to connect with Australia’s AI builders, researchers and entrepreneurs? Explore MLAI’s events and partnership opportunities.”  
> Target: `/events` or `/sponsors`

**For partners/sponsors:**
> “Partner with MLAI to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs building the future of AI.”  
> Target: `/sponsors`

### CTA Rules

- Use **ONE CTA per article**, ideally as a natural final line or short aside.
- Match the CTA to the article topic:
  - Learning/general AI → `/events`
  - Building/prototyping → `/hackathons`
  - Healthcare AI → `/medhack`
  - Community participation/rewards → `/roo`
  - Sponsorship/partnership → `/sponsors`
- Keep CTAs helpful and community-oriented, not sales-heavy.
- Avoid implying a guaranteed outcome from attending, joining, sponsoring or participating.
- Use MLAI’s own terminology: “events,” “hackathons,” “community,” “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “Roo Points,” “Slack,” “perks,” “practical AI applications.”
- When referencing Founder Tools, avoid inventing a URL unless confirmed. Use a generic CTA or mark the URL as [To be filled].

### What NOT to do with CTAs

- Do not use aggressive sales language such as “Buy now,” “Dominate AI,” “Get guaranteed investors,” or “Secure funding today.”
- Do not push Roo Points as a paid currency, job pathway or income opportunity.
- Do not promise mentorship, paid work, sponsorship, investor introductions or prizes unless specifically tied to an event page.
- Do not direct every reader to sponsor pages; sponsorship CTAs are only for partner/business-leader content.
- Do not position MLAI as providing professional advice, medical advice, fundraising advice or investment advice.

---

## 7. Tone & Voice Guidelines

**Brand Voice:** Practical, community-first, builder-friendly, optimistic and accessible. MLAI’s tone is energetic and inviting, but should stay grounded in real-world outcomes rather than hype.

### Tone Attributes

- **Practical over hype:** Focus on building, learning, prototyping and solving real problems.
- **Community-first:** Emphasise collaboration, contribution, participation and shared progress.
- **Accessible but credible:** Make AI feel approachable without oversimplifying technical or sector-specific issues.
- **Optimistic and action-oriented:** Encourage readers to get involved, meet people, build something and contribute.
- **Cross-disciplinary:** Welcome non-technical experts as well as technical builders.
- **Australian ecosystem-focused:** Frame MLAI as supporting Australia’s AI and machine learning community.

### Do’s

- Use inclusive language such as “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “students,” “clinicians,” “researchers,” “mentors” and “community.”
- Use practical phrases from the site, such as:
  - “real-world challenges”
  - “practical AI applications”
  - “meaningful professional connections”
  - “help the community”
  - “get involved”
  - “build practical AI and software projects”
  - “real solutions to real bottlenecks”
- Use Australian English spelling where applicable: “organisation,” “recognise,” “programs” is acceptable because the site uses “programs,” “optimise” where relevant.
- Explain AI concepts in plain English before going deeper.
- Keep article advice educational and informational unless MLAI has explicitly agreed to provide professional services in that context.
- When writing about hackathons, emphasise teamwork, mentors, prototypes, judging criteria and real-world problem solving.
- When writing about Roo Points, include the internal-use caveat: they are recognition points, not money or wages.

### Don’ts

- Don’t use inflated AI hype such as “revolutionary,” “world-changing,” “unprecedented,” or “disrupt everything” unless quoting event copy directly.
- Don’t imply that AI alone solves complex healthcare, energy, safety or policy problems.
- Don’t make unsupported claims about event attendance, sponsor names, awards, university affiliations or participant outcomes.
- Don’t treat MLAI like a commercial SaaS company first; its primary identity is community/not-for-profit.
- Don’t overuse corporate jargon such as “synergise,” “unlock exponential value,” or “leverage AI transformation” without concrete meaning.
- Don’t write as if MLAI provides legal, financial, investment, medical or fundraising advice.
- Don’t frame community participation as transactional labour or employment.
- Don’t refer to Roo Points as cryptocurrency, tokens, cash, salary, wages or a marketplace currency.

---

## 8. Writing Guidance Summary

### Language Conventions

- **Locale:** Australian English preferred.
- **Preferred terms:** MLAI, MLAI Aus, Australian AI community, AI and machine learning, builders, learners, founders, entrepreneurs, researchers, students, clinicians, practitioners, community, events, meetups, workshops, hackathons, Roo, Roo Points, Slack, perks, founder updates, investor network.
- **Avoided terms:** Guaranteed funding, guaranteed jobs, guaranteed investment, cash value, wage, salary, cryptocurrency, official endorsement, professional advice, medical advice, legal advice, investment advice, “AI will replace,” “AI solves everything.”

### Content Rules

- Present MLAI as an Australian not-for-profit AI and machine learning community, not primarily as a SaaS vendor, agency, accelerator or investment platform.
- Ground articles in practical value: what readers can learn, build, contribute or connect around.
- Use proof points sparingly. The strongest available statistic is “over 10,000 members,” but avoid repeating it in every article.
- If discussing MedHack, describe it as a healthcare AI hackathon bringing clinicians, builders and optimists together to prototype solutions to healthcare bottlenecks.
- If discussing Roo, always clarify that Roo Points are an internal thank-you/contribution recognition system and have no cash value.
- If discussing sponsors, position sponsorship as supporting Australia’s AI community and connecting with talented developers, researchers and entrepreneurs.
- If discussing founder support, focus on early investor-network building, founder updates and sharpening signal. Do not promise fundraising success.
- Do not imply MLAI guarantees outcomes from events, programs, hackathons, sponsorships or community participation.
- Avoid inventing program details, dates, prizes, sponsor names, mentor names or URLs not present in source content.
- Keep CTAs soft, relevant and community-aligned: invite readers to explore events, hackathons, Roo or sponsorship pages depending on the article topic.

pillars.name: Practical AI Learning in Australia
pillars.description: A pillar focused on making AI and machine learning more accessible through beginner-friendly, hands-on learning pathways, workshops, meetups and community-supported education. This topic supports learners, students and early-career practitioners who want to move beyond AI hype and understand how to build practical skills in an Australian context.
pillars.content_angle: Make AI feel approachable by framing learning around community, hands-on practice, meetups, workshops and real-world examples rather than abstract theory or hype.

pillars.name: AI Hackathons and Real-World Prototyping
pillars.description: A pillar covering how AI and software hackathons help builders, students, researchers and domain experts collaborate on real-world challenges. It can support cluster content on hackathon preparation, judging criteria, team formation, prototyping, mentoring and sector-specific innovation challenges such as healthcare, energy and online safety.
pillars.content_angle: Position hackathons as practical, collaborative environments where technical and non-technical participants can work together on useful prototypes for real problems.
seed_keywords: how to prepare for a hackathon

pillars.name: AI Startup Founder Support and Investor Readiness
pillars.description: A pillar for AI founders and entrepreneurs who need to build stronger networks, communicate progress clearly and prepare before fundraising conversations begin. This topic connects to MLAI’s founder tools, founder updates and community support while staying educational and avoiding promises of funding or investment outcomes.
pillars.content_angle: Help founders understand why investor-network building and clear founder updates should start early, with community support as a practical advantage rather than a guaranteed funding pathway.

pillars.name: AI Community Building and Professional Connections
pillars.description: A pillar focused on the value of local AI communities, meetups, Slack participation, volunteering and meaningful professional connections. It addresses the common pain point of builders, learners, founders and researchers lacking a supportive local network where they can meet collaborators, mentors and peers.
pillars.content_angle: Show community as a practical part of learning and building with AI, not just a networking extra, by emphasising contribution, collaboration and shared progress.

pillars.name: Applied AI for Healthcare, Energy and Safety
pillars.description: A pillar exploring practical AI applications in sectors where domain experts and technical builders can collaborate on real bottlenecks. This aligns with MLAI’s hackathon examples including MedHack: Frontiers, Watt The Hack and the eSafety Hackathon, while keeping content educational and avoiding professional advice claims.
pillars.content_angle: Bridge the gap between technical AI capability and sector expertise by focusing on collaborative problem framing, practical prototypes and responsible real-world application.

pillars.name: Community Contribution, Roo Points and Participation
pillars.description: A pillar explaining how people can get more involved in the MLAI community through Slack participation, volunteering, useful contributions and Roo-managed recognition. It can support content on community norms, contribution ideas, perks and how Roo Points work as internal thank-you points with no cash value.
pillars.content_angle: Frame contribution as a community-first way to participate, help others and be recognised, while clearly distinguishing Roo Points from money, wages or employment compensation.
seed_keywords: how to get involved in AI community

pillars.name: AI Ecosystem Sponsorship and Partnerships
pillars.description: A pillar for organisations, sponsors and ecosystem leaders interested in supporting Australia’s AI community and connecting with builders, researchers, founders and entrepreneurs. It can support cluster content on ethical community sponsorship, event partnerships, ecosystem engagement and credible ways to support practical AI initiatives.
pillars.content_angle: Position sponsorship as a credible way to support community-led AI ecosystem growth and connect with Australian AI talent without overclaiming endorsement or guaranteed outcomes.

pillar_strategy.rationale: These pillars were chosen because they map directly to MLAI’s core audiences and offerings: practical AI learning through events and workshops, hackathons for real-world prototyping, founder support, community participation, applied sector challenges, contribution recognition through Roo Points and sponsorship opportunities. Together, they create a topic cluster architecture that reflects MLAI’s not-for-profit, community-first role in Australia’s AI and machine learning ecosystem while supporting both informational and commercial search intent.

{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",
    "logrocket": "^10.1.1",

{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",
    "logrocket": "^10.1.1",
    "
